column1: How was it detected? It wasn't the Ministry itself that discovered it

column1: The key contradiction: The Ministry claims there is no evidence of intrusion

column2: 500 times the monthly minimum wage the main and most serious crime in the case";"Layer 1 — Authentication (counters credential stuffing): The most critical measure in this case. If the Tax Agency had implemented MFA (two-factor authentication)
column3: the credentials stolen from previous leaks would have been useless. The attacker would have the password but not the second factor. Additionally
column4: tools like HaveIBeenPwned allow for automatic verification of whether employee credentials have appeared in previous leaks.

column1: Layer 2 — Access Control (counters IDOR): This is the direct correction of the technical vulnerability used. The rule is simple: before returning any data
column2: the system must verify that the person requesting the record with ID number X has permission to view that record. If they don't
column3: the request is denied. It seems obvious
column4: but it's one of the most common errors in legacy government systems.

column1: Layer 3 — Monitoring: A system that detects the same IP address querying millions of records sequentially must automatically block access. The IDOR attack involves enumerating IDs one by one
column2: generating a highly recognizable traffic pattern in the logs.

column1: Layer 4 — Encryption: Even if the attacker manages to extract the database
column2: if the data is encrypted at rest
column3: all they get is unreadable garbage. This doesn't prevent theft
column4: but it does eliminate the value of the stolen data.

column1: Layer 5 — Response: Having a defined protocol before the incident occurs reduces reaction time from days to hours
column2: limits the damage
column3: and complies with the GDPR's 72-hour notification requirements.";https://elpais.com/economia/2026-02-02/hacienda-investiga-un-posible-hackeo-que-compromete-datos-personales-bancarios-y-fiscales-de-ciudadanos.html;This case is especially valuable for digital forensics because it brings together several critical elements commonly found in real-world incidents: an alleged massive data breach
column4: a publicly identified attacker
column5: information disseminated on forums and social media
column6: and an institution that initially denies having been compromised. This combination forces investigators to work on multiple fronts simultaneously
column7: applying technical analysis of systems and logs
column8: OSINT techniques to track the actor and verify the published information
column9: and formal processes for collecting and preserving digital evidence to ensure its legal validity.;"Key evidence to collect:

column1: System logs: database accesses
column2: failed authentication attempts
column3: and unusual server activity.

column1: Network traffic: to identify potential massive data transfers or suspicious external connections.
Copies of compromised databases: to compare the leaked data with the originals.
Forum and social media posts: as part of OSINT analysis
column2: to trace the attacker and verify the authenticity of the leak.

column1: Devices and user accounts with privileged access: to rule out or confirm misuse of internal credentials.
All this evidence must be collected following the Chain of Custody protocol to ensure its legal admissibility in an investigation or legal proceeding.";Juan Guillermo Cardona Cifuentes

column3: the WannaCry ransomware worm spread to more than 200

column1: Within hours of the attack
column2: WannaCry was temporarily neutralized. A security researcher discovered a ""kill switch"" that essentially disabled the malware. However
column3: many affected computers remained encrypted and unusable until victims paid the ransom or were able to reverse the encryption.

column1: WannaCry spread by exploiting a vulnerability called ""EternalBlue."" The US National Security Agency (NSA) had developed this exploit
column2: supposedly for its own use
column3: but a group known as Shadow Brokers stole it and made it public after the NSA itself was compromised. EternalBlue only worked on older
column4: unpatched versions of Microsoft Windows
column5: but there were many machines still running those versions
column6: allowing WannaCry to spread rapidly.

column1: ;Friday May 12 -2017;On the day of the attack
column2: Marcus Hutchins
column3: a blogger and security researcher
column4: began reverse engineering WannaCry's source code. He discovered that WannaCry included an unusual feature: before running
column5: it performed a query for the domain iuqerfsodp9ifjaposdfjhgosurijfaewrwergwea.com. This website did not exist.

column1: So he registered the domain. (It costs $10.69).
After Hutchins did this
column2: copies of WannaCry continued to spread
column3: but stopped running. Basically
column4: WannaCry stopped working on its own once it started receiving a response from iuqerfsodp9ifjaposdfjhgosurijfaewrwergwea.com."; law Budapest Convention on Cybercrime;Art. 269  malicious software.;"However
column5: if the malware sends its test query to a hardcoded domain
column6: if someone registers the domain
column7: they can be tricked into thinking they are always in a secure space. Perhaps this happened with WannaCry: copies of WannaCry around the world were tricked into thinking they were in a secure space
column8: and were disabled. (A better design
column9: from the malware author's point of view
column10: would be to query a random domain that was different each time; that way the chances of getting a response from the domain outside of a safe space would be close to zero.)

column1: Another possible explanation is that the copy of WannaCry that spread around the world was unfinished. The WannaCry authors may have used hardcode in that domain as a marker
column2: intending to replace it with the address of their command and control (C&C) server before releasing the worm. Or they may have wanted to register iuqerfsodp9ifjaposdfjhgosurijfaewrwergwea.com themselves. (Perhaps DNS filtering or URL filtering could have stopped queries to that domain
column3: but most organizations would not have been able to implement this security measure in time.)

column1: Regardless of the reason
column2: it was fortunate that such a simple action could save computers and networks around the world from further infections.";https://www.cloudflare.com/es-es/learning/security/ransomware/wannacry-ransomware/;"The version of WannaCry that spread in 2017 no longer works
column3: thanks to Hutchins' mastery of the kill button. Additionally
column4: a patch is available for the EternalBlue vulnerability that WannaCry had been exploiting since March 2017.

column1: However
column2: WannaCry attacks continue to occur. As of March 2021
column3: WannaCry was still exploiting the EternalBlue vulnerability
column4: meaning that only extremely old and outdated Windows systems were at risk. Newer versions of WannaCry have removed the kill button feature that was in the original version. It is strongly recommended that operating systems be updated and security updates installed immediately.

Training staff in cybersecurity best practices is also crucial.;https://finance.yahoo.com/news/hackers-roban-identidades-con-el-205814146.html ;Digital forensics plays a crucial role in analyzing attacks, determining how they occurred, identifying those responsible, and recovering digital evidence. 27;INVIMA (Rasonmware);Colombia;Ransomware (BlackByte variant);The attack was carried out using BlackByte ransomware, designed to encrypt valuable data and critical infrastructure, then demand a ransom payment from the victim to restore access.;February 2022;The Invima attack took place amid a regional surge in ransomware activity across Latin America in early 2022, a wave that heavily impacted Colombia one of the hardest-hit countries affecting sectors such as air transportation (Viva Air), financial services, and government institutions, with groups like Lockbit, BlackByte, and RansomEXX behind many incidents.;Article 58, numeral 17 (modified by Law 1273 of 2009);Under Law 1273 of 2009, the crime of interference in a computer system (Article 269I) carries penalties of 48 to 96 months in prison and fines of 100 to 1,000 times the minimum monthly wage. Penalties increase if the conduct affects state or national defense systems.;Following the 2022 wave of attacks, cybersecurity experts recommend implementing constant system updates with the latest security patches, deploying multifactor authentication (MFA) for all remote access and cloud services, performing regular backups stored offline to ensure availability, segmenting networks to isolate critical systems and prevent lateral movement, and maintaining 24/7 monitoring to detect anomalous activity.;https://www.bluradio.com/nacion/ciberataque-al-invima-fue-ocasionado-por-ransomware-blackbyte;The investigation of the cyberattack on Invima was taken over by the Cyber Center of the National Police. Forensic computing played a crucial role in the process, as digital evidence was collected and analyzed in specialized laboratories of the Technical Investigation Corps (CTI) of the Prosecutor's Office.;In a ransomware attack like the one against Invima, key digital evidence to collect includes samples of the malware (such as BlackByte) for technical analysis, forensic images of compromised servers, system logs to trace the attack vector, IP addresses of the attackers (which in this case pointed to servers in Russia, China, Venezuela, and Nicaragua), encrypted files as proof of the impact, and offline backups to enable system recovery without paying the ransom.;Cristian Alejandro Pinzón Marentes
